    27→/-- The number of pairs of points of a finite set `s` in a metric space that are distance 1 apart.

    15→-/
    16→module
    17→
    18→public import Mathlib.LinearAlgebra.Orientation
    19→public import Mathlib.Analysis.InnerProductSpace.PiL2
    20→public import Mathlib.Geometry.Euclidean.Angle.Oriented.Affine
    21→public import Mathlib.Geometry.Euclidean.Triangle
    22→
    23→public import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Logic.Equiv.Fin.Rotate
    24→public import FormalConjecturesForMathlib.Data.Set.Triplewise
    25→
    26→@[expose] public section
    27→
    28→scoped[EuclideanGeometry] notation "ℝ²" => EuclideanSpace ℝ (Fin 2)
    29→
    30→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    31→
    32→/-- Oriented angles make sense in 2d.
    33→
    34→Note: this can't blindly be added to mathlib as it creates an "instance diamond"
    35→with an instance for modules satisfying `is_empty`. -/
    36→noncomputable instance Module.orientedEuclideanSpaceFinTwo : Module.Oriented ℝ ℝ² (Fin 2) :=
    37→  ⟨Basis.orientation <| PiLp.basisFun 2 _ _⟩
    38→
    39→/-- Two dimensional euclidean space is two-dimensional. -/
    40→instance fact_finrank_euclideanSpace_fin_two : Fact (Module.finrank ℝ ℝ² = 2) :=
    41→  ⟨finrank_euclideanSpace_fin⟩
    42→
    43→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    44→
    45→open scoped Real
    46→
    47→namespace EuclideanGeometry
    48→
    49→variable {V P : Type*} {n : ℕ}
    50→
    51→variable [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V] [MetricSpace P] [NormedAddTorsor V P]
    52→
    53→variable [Module.Oriented ℝ V (Fin 2)] [Fact (Module.finrank ℝ V = 2)] {p : Fin n → P}
    54→
    55→/-- We say a subset `A` of points in the plane is non-trilinear
    56→if it contains no three points that lie on the same line. -/
    57→def NonTrilinear (A : Set P) : Prop :=
    58→  A.Triplewise (fun x y z ↦ ¬ Collinear ℝ {x, y, z})
    59→
    60→/-- `ConvexIndep S` means that `S` consists of extremal points of its convex hull,
    61→i.e., the point set encloses a convex shape.
    62→Also known as a "convex-independent set". -/
    63→def ConvexIndep (S : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    64→  ∀ a ∈ S, a ∉ convexHull ℝ (S \ {a})
    65→
    66→/-- The set `P` contains a convex `n`-gon.
    67→See also `IsConvexPolygon`. -/
    68→def HasConvexNGon (n : ℕ) (P : Set ℝ²) : Prop :=
    69→  ∃ S : Finset ℝ², S.card = n ∧ ↑S ⊆ P ∧ ConvexIndep S
    70→
    71→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a counter-clockwise convex polygon. -/
    72→def IsCcwConvexPolygon (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
    73→  ∀ ⦃i j k⦄, i < j → j < k → (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1
    74→
    75→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    76→  (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p i) (p j) (p k)).sign = 1 := hp hij hjk
    77→
    78→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    79→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    80→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p j) (p k) (p i)).sign = 1 := by
    81→  rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    82→  exact hp hij hjk
    83→
    84→set_option linter.docPrime false in
    85→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle'' (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p) {i j k : Fin n}
    86→    (hij : i < j) (hjk : j < k) : (∡ (p k) (p i) (p j)).sign = 1 := by
    87→  rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
    88→  exact hp hij hjk
    89→
    90→theorem IsCcwConvexPolygon.sign_oangle_finRotate (hp : IsCcwConvexPolygon p)
    91→    (hn : 3 ≤ n) (i : Fin n) :
    92→    (∡ (p i) (p <| finRotate _ i) (p <| finRotate _ (finRotate _ i))).sign = 1 := by
    93→  obtain ⟨n, rfl⟩ := le_iff_exists_add'.mp hn
    94→  by_cases hi : i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    95→  · rw [hi, finRotate_last, finRotate_apply_zero]
    96→    exact hp.sign_oangle'' Fin.zero_lt_one Fin.one_lt_last
    97→  by_cases hi' : finRotate _ i = Fin.last (n + 2)
    98→  · rw [hi', finRotate_last]
    99→    refine hp.sign_oangle' ?_ ((Fin.le_last _).lt_of_ne hi)
   100→    rw [Fin.pos_iff_ne_zero]
   101→    rintro rfl
   102→    rw [finRotate_apply_zero] at hi'
   103→    exact Fin.one_lt_last.ne hi'
   104→  apply hp.sign_oangle <;> apply lt_finRotate_of_ne_last <;> assumption
   105→
   106→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_zero (p : Fin 0 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := finZeroElim
   107→
   108→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_one (p : Fin 1 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   109→
   110→@[simp] theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_two (p : Fin 2 → P) : IsCcwConvexPolygon p := by intro; omega
   111→
   112→set_option linter.docPrime false in
   113→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four' {p : Fin 4 → P} :
   114→    IsCcwConvexPolygon p ↔ (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 1) (p 2) (p 3)).sign = 1 ∧
   115→    (∡ (p 2) (p 3) (p 0)).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ (p 3) (p 0) (p 1)).sign = 1 := by
   116→  refine ⟨fun h ↦ ?_, fun ⟨h1, h2, h3, h4⟩ ↦ ?_⟩
   117→  · obtain ⟨h01, h12, h23⟩ : (0 : Fin 4) < 1 ∧ (1 : Fin 4) < 2 ∧ (2 : Fin 4) < 3 := by simp
   118→    · repeat' constructor
   119→      · exact h.sign_oangle h01 h12
   120→      · exact h.sign_oangle h12 h23
   121→      · exact h.sign_oangle' (h01.trans h12) h23
   122→      · exact h.sign_oangle'' h01 (h12.trans h23)
   123→  · intro i j k hij hjk
   124→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   125→    · exact h1
   126→    · rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   127→      exact h4
   128→    · rw [← EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rotate_sign]
   129→      exact h3
   130→    · exact h2
   131→
   132→@[simp]
   133→theorem isCcwConvexPolygon_four (A B C D : P) :
   134→    IsCcwConvexPolygon ![A, B, C, D] ↔
   135→      (∡ A B C).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ B C D).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ C D A).sign = 1 ∧ (∡ D A B).sign = 1 :=
   136→  isCcwConvexPolygon_four'
   137→
   138→/-- The statement that a sequence of points form a convex polygon. -/
   139→def IsConvexPolygon {n : ℕ} (p : Fin n → P) : Prop :=
   140→  IsCcwConvexPolygon p ∨ IsCcwConvexPolygon fun i => p (-i)
   141→
   142→/-- Three affine independent points always form a convex polygon. -/
   143→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent {A B C : P}
   144→    (hABC : AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C]) : IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] := by
   145→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff] at hABC
   146→  cases hsABC : (∡ A B C).sign
   147→  · exact (hABC hsABC).elim
   148→  · right
   149→    intro i j k hij hjk
   150→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   151→    rw [EuclideanGeometry.oangle_rev, Real.Angle.sign_neg, neg_eq_iff_eq_neg]
   152→    exact (oangle_rotate_sign A B C).trans hsABC
   153→  · left
   154→    intro i j k hij hjk
   155→    fin_cases i <;> fin_cases j <;> fin_cases k <;> simp at hij hjk
   156→    exact hsABC
   157→
   158→theorem isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent {A B C : P} :
   159→    IsConvexPolygon ![A, B, C] ↔ AffineIndependent ℝ ![A, B, C] := by
   160→  refine ⟨fun h => ?_, isConvexPolygon_three_of_affineIndependent⟩
   161→  rw [← oangle_ne_zero_and_ne_pi_iff_affineIndependent, ← Real.Angle.sign_ne_zero_iff]
   162→  let p := ![A, B, C]
   163→  change IsConvexPolygon p at h
   164→  change Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 1) (p 2)) ≠ 0
   165→  cases h with
   166→  | inl h =>
   167→    rw [h.sign_oangle (by simp) (by simp)]
   168→    rintro ⟨⟩
   169→  | inr h =>
   170→    suffices Real.Angle.sign (∡ (p 0) (p 2) (p 1)) = 1 by rw [← oangle_swap₂₃_sign, this]; rintro ⟨⟩
   171→    exact h.sign_oangle (i := 0) (j := 1) (k := 2) (by simp) (by simp)
   172→
   173→theorem isConvexPolygon_triangle (t : Affine.Triangle ℝ P) : IsConvexPolygon t.points := by
   174→  have : t.points = ![t.points 0, t.points 1, t.points 2] := by ext i; fin_cases i <;> rfl
   175→  rw [this, isConvexPolygon_three_iff_affineIndependent, ← this]
   176→  exact t.independent
   177→
   178→noncomputable def triangle_area (a b c : P) : ℝ :=
   179→  positiveOrientation.areaForm (a -ᵥ c) (b -ᵥ c) / 2
   180→
   181→lemma triangle_area_eq_det (a b c : ℝ²) :
   182→    triangle_area a b c =
   183→    Matrix.det !![a 0, b 0, c 0;
   184→                  a 1, b 1, c 1;
   185→                  1,   1,   1] / 2 := by
   186→  rw [triangle_area, Orientation.areaForm_to_volumeForm,
   187→    positiveOrientation.volumeForm_robust (EuclideanSpace.basisFun (Fin 2) ℝ) rfl, Module.Basis.det_apply]
   188→  suffices (a 0 - c 0) * (b 1 - c 1) - (b 0 - c 0) * (a 1 - c 1) =
   189→      a 0 * b 1 - a 0 * c 1 - b 0 * a 1 + b 0 * c 1 + c 0 * a 1 - c 0 * b 1 by
   190→    simp [Matrix.det_fin_two, Matrix.det_fin_three, Module.Basis.toMatrix, this]
   191→  ring
   192→
   193→/--
   194→Given a finite set of points in the plane, we define the number of distinct distances between pairs
   195→of points.
   196→-/
   197→noncomputable def distinctDistances (points : Finset ℝ²) : ℕ :=
   198→  (points.offDiag.image fun (pair : ℝ² × ℝ²) => dist pair.1 pair.2).card
   199→
   200→
   201→/-- Given a finite set of points in the, we define the number of distinct distances between
   202→a given point and all other points -/
   203→noncomputable def distinctDistancesFrom (points : Finset ℝ²) (pt : ℝ²) : ℕ :=
   204→  (points.image fun x => dist x pt).card
   205→
   206→end EuclideanGeometry
   207→
   208→
   209→def IsIsosceles {α : Type*} [Dist α] (p q r : α) : Prop :=
   210→  dist p q = dist q r ∨ dist q r = dist r p ∨ dist r p = dist p q
   211→
   212→nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {α : Type} [Dist α] (A : Set α) :=
   213→  Nonempty A ∧ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles · · ·)
   214→

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 165
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/165](https://www.erdosproblems.com/165)
    23→
    24→Give an asymptotic formula for $R(3,k)$. Prize: $250.
    25→
    26→$R(3,k)$ is the Ramsey number: the minimum $N$ such that every simple graph
    27→on $N$ vertices contains either a triangle ($3$-clique) or an independent set
    28→of size $k$ (equivalently, a $k$-clique in the complement).
    29→
    30→It is known that for some constant $c > 0$ and large $k$:
    31→$$
    32→  (c + o(1)) \frac{k^2}{\log k} \leq R(3,k) \leq (1 + o(1)) \frac{k^2}{\log k}
    33→$$
    34→
    35→The upper bound is due to Shearer [Sh83], improving Ajtai–Komlós–Szemerédi
    36→[AKS80]. The lower bound has been progressively improved: Kim [Ki95] showed
    37→$c \geq 1/162$, Fiz Pontiveros–Griffiths–Morris [PGM20] and Bohman–Keevash
    38→[BoKe21] improved this to $c \geq 1/4$, Campos–Jenssen–Michelen–Sahasrabudhe
    39→[CJMS25] to $c \geq 1/3$, and Hefty–Horn–King–Pfender [HHKP25] to
    40→$c \geq 1/2$. The conjectured asymptotic is
    41→$R(3,k) \sim \frac{1}{2} \frac{k^2}{\log k}$.
    42→
    43→Related problems: 544, 986, 1013. OEIS: [A000791](https://oeis.org/A000791).
    44→
    45→[AKS80] Ajtai, M., Komlós, J. and Szemerédi, E., _A note on Ramsey numbers_.
    46→J. Combin. Theory Ser. A **29** (1980), 354-360.
    47→
    48→[Sh83] Shearer, J. B., _A note on the independence number of triangle-free
    49→graphs_. Discrete Math. **46** (1983), 83-87.
    50→
    51→[Ki95] Kim, J. H., _The Ramsey number R(3,t) has order of magnitude t²/log t_.
    52→Random Structures and Algorithms (1995), 173-207.
    53→
    54→[PGM20] Fiz Pontiveros, G., Griffiths, S. and Morris, R., _The triangle-free
    55→process and the Ramsey number R(3,k)_. Mem. Amer. Math. Soc. (2020).
    56→
    57→[BoKe21] Bohman, T. and Keevash, P., _Dynamic concentration of the
    58→triangle-free process_. Random Structures Algorithms (2021), 221-293.
    59→
    60→[CJMS25] Campos, M., Jenssen, M., Michelen, M. and Sahasrabudhe, J.,
    61→_A new lower bound for the Ramsey numbers R(3,k)_. arXiv:2505.13371 (2025).
    62→
    63→[HHKP25] Hefty, L., Horn, P., King, R. and Pfender, F., _Improving R(3,k) in
    64→just two bites_. arXiv:2510.19718 (2025).
    65→
    66→[Er61] Erdős, P. (1961). [Er71] Erdős, P. (1971). [Er78] Erdős, P. (1978).
    67→[Er90b] Erdős, P. (1990). [Er93] Erdős, P. (1993). [Er97c] Erdős, P. (1997).
    68→-/
    69→
    70→open SimpleGraph Real
    71→
    72→namespace Erdos165
    73→
    74→/-- The Ramsey number $R(3,k)$: the minimum $N$ such that every simple graph
    75→on $N$ vertices contains either a triangle ($3$-clique) or an independent
    76→set of size $k$ (a $k$-clique in the complement). -/
    77→noncomputable def ramseyR3 (k : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    78→  sInf {N : ℕ | ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin N)), ¬G.CliqueFree 3 ∨ ¬Gᶜ.CliqueFree k}
    79→
    80→/--
    81→Erdős Conjecture (Problem 165) [Er61, Er71, Er90b, Er93, Er97c]:
    82→
    83→There exists a constant $c > 0$ such that $R(3,k) \sim c \cdot k^2 / \log k$, i.e.,
    84→for all $\varepsilon > 0$ and all sufficiently large $k$:
    85→$$
    86→  (c - \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{k^2}{\log k} \leq R(3,k) \leq (c + \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{k^2}{\log k}.
    87→$$
    88→
    89→The conjectured value is $c = 1/2$.
    90→-/
    91→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    92→theorem erdos_165 : answer(sorry) ↔
    93→    ∃ c : ℝ, 0 < c ∧ ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
    94→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, N₀ ≤ k →
    95→      (c - ε) * ((k : ℝ) ^ 2 / Real.log (k : ℝ)) ≤ (ramseyR3 k : ℝ) ∧
    96→      (ramseyR3 k : ℝ) ≤ (c + ε) * ((k : ℝ) ^ 2 / Real.log (k : ℝ)) := by
    97→  sorry
    98→
    99→/--
   100→Erdős Problem 165 — conjectured value $c = 1/2$:
   101→
   102→$R(3,k) \sim \frac{1}{2} \frac{k^2}{\log k}$, i.e., for all $\varepsilon > 0$
   103→and all sufficiently large $k$:
   104→$$
   105→  (1/2 - \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{k^2}{\log k} \leq R(3,k) \leq (1/2 + \varepsilon) \cdot \frac{k^2}{\log k}.
   106→$$
   107→-/
   108→@[category research open, AMS 5]
   109→theorem erdos_165_conjectured_value :
   110→    ∀ ε : ℝ, 0 < ε →
   111→    ∃ N₀ : ℕ, ∀ k : ℕ, N₀ ≤ k →
   112→      (1 / 2 - ε) * ((k : ℝ) ^ 2 / Real.log (k : ℝ)) ≤ (ramseyR3 k : ℝ) ∧
   113→      (ramseyR3 k : ℝ) ≤ (1 / 2 + ε) * ((k : ℝ) ^ 2 / Real.log (k : ℝ)) := by
   114→  sorry
   115→
   116→end Erdos165

    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 118
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/118](https://www.erdosproblems.com/118)
    23→
    24→Erdős–Hajnal conjecture on partition ordinals (disproved): if $\alpha \to (\alpha, 3)^2$, does
    25→$\alpha \to (\alpha, n)^2$ hold for all $n \geq 3$? Independently disproved by Schipperus and
    26→Darby.
    27→
    28→[Er87] Erdős, P., *Some of my favourite problems in various branches of combinatorics*.
    29→
    30→[Er90] Erdős, P., *Some of my favourite problems in number theory, combinatorics, and geometry*.
    31→
    32→[Er95d] Erdős, P., *Problems and results on finite and infinite combinatorial analysis*.
    33→
    34→[Er97f] Erdős, P., *Some problems on finite and infinite graphs*.
    35→
    36→[Sc99] Schipperus, R., *Countable partition ordinals*, PhD thesis, University of Colorado
    37→at Boulder, 1999.
    38→
    39→[Sc10] Schipperus, R., *Countable partition ordinals*, Ann. Pure Appl. Logic 161 (2010),
    40→no. 10, 1195–1215.
    41→
    42→[Da99] Darby, C., *Negative partition relations for ordinals $\omega^{\omega^\alpha}$*,
    43→J. Combin. Theory Ser. B (1999), 205–222.
    44→
    45→[La00] Larson, J.A., *An ordinal partition avoiding pentagrams*, J. Symbolic Logic (2000),
    46→969–978.
    47→
    48→[HST10] Hajnal, A. and Larson, J.A., *Partition relations*, in: Handbook of Set Theory,
    49→Springer, 2010.
    50→
    51→See also Erdős problems 590, 591, 592.
    52→-/
    53→
    54→open Ordinal
    55→
    56→namespace Erdos118
    57→
    58→/-- The "underlying type" of ordinal $\alpha$: the set of all ordinals strictly less
    59→than $\alpha$, linearly ordered by the natural ordering on ordinals. -/
    60→abbrev OrdinalSet (α : Ordinal) := {a : Ordinal // a < α}
    61→
    62→/-- The ordinal partition relation $\alpha \to (\beta, \gamma)^2$:
    63→every $2$-coloring of the pairs of elements of $\alpha$ (viewed as the set of
    64→ordinals less than $\alpha$) contains either:
    65→- an order-embedded copy of $\beta$ whose pairs are all colored $0$ (red), or
    66→- an order-embedded copy of $\gamma$ whose pairs are all colored $1$ (blue).
    67→
    68→Here a "copy of $\beta$" is given by an order embedding
    69→$e : \operatorname{OrdinalSet}(\beta) \hookrightarrow \operatorname{OrdinalSet}(\alpha)$,
    70→and monochromaticity means $f(e(i), e(j)) = c$ for all $i < j$. -/
    71→def OrdPartition (α β γ : Ordinal) : Prop :=
    72→  ∀ (f : OrdinalSet α → OrdinalSet α → Fin 2),
    73→    (∃ e : OrdinalSet β ↪o OrdinalSet α,
    74→      ∀ i j : OrdinalSet β, i < j → f (e i) (e j) = 0) ∨
    75→    (∃ e : OrdinalSet γ ↪o OrdinalSet α,
    76→      ∀ i j : OrdinalSet γ, i < j → f (e i) (e j) = 1)
    77→
    78→/--
    79→Erdős–Hajnal Conjecture on Partition Ordinals (Problem 118)
    80→[Er87, Er90, Er95d, Er97f] — **DISPROVED**
    81→
    82→An ordinal $\alpha$ is called a *partition ordinal* if $\alpha \to (\alpha, 3)^2$, i.e., every
    83→$2$-coloring of pairs from a linearly ordered set of order type $\alpha$ contains either
    84→a monochromatic copy of $\alpha$ in color $0$ (red) or a monochromatic triangle $K_3$ in
    85→color $1$ (blue).
    86→
    87→Erdős and Hajnal conjectured that for every partition ordinal $\alpha$ and every $n \geq 3$,
    88→we also have $\alpha \to (\alpha, n)^2$.
    89→
    90→This conjecture is FALSE, as independently shown by Schipperus [Sc99] (published in
    91→[Sc10]) and Darby [Da99]. For example, Larson [La00] showed that $\omega^{\omega^2}$ is a
    92→partition ordinal — i.e., $\omega^{\omega^2} \to (\omega^{\omega^2}, 3)^2$ holds — but
    93→$\omega^{\omega^2} \to (\omega^{\omega^2}, 5)^2$ fails.
    94→
    95→See also Hajnal–Larson, Chapter 2.9 of [HST10] for background and proof sketches.
    96→-/
    97→@[category research solved, AMS 3 5]
    98→theorem erdos_118 : answer(False) ↔ ∀ (α : Ordinal), OrdPartition α α 3 →
    99→      ∀ n : ℕ, 3 ≤ n → OrdPartition α α (↑n) := by
   100→  sorry
   101→
   102→/--
   103→Explicit counterexample to the Erdős–Hajnal conjecture (Problem 118):
   104→$\omega^{\omega^2} \to (\omega^{\omega^2}, 3)^2$ holds (i.e., $\omega^{\omega^2}$ is a
   105→partition ordinal), but $\omega^{\omega^2} \to (\omega^{\omega^2}, 5)^2$ fails.
   106→
   107→This was shown by Larson [La00] (the partition ordinal property) and the failure for $n = 5$
   108→follows from the counterexamples of Schipperus [Sc99, Sc10] and Darby [Da99].
   109→-/
   110→@[category research solved, AMS 3 5]
   111→theorem erdos_118_counterexample :
   112→    OrdPartition (ω ^ (ω ^ 2)) (ω ^ (ω ^ 2)) 3 ∧
   113→    ¬ OrdPartition (ω ^ (ω ^ 2)) (ω ^ (ω ^ 2)) 5 := by
   114→  sorry
   115→
   116→end Erdos118
   117→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 151
    22→
    23→Erdős and Gallai conjectured that the clique transversal number of any graph on $n$ vertices
    24→is at most $n - H(n)$, where $H(n)$ is the minimum independence number over all triangle-free
    25→graphs on $n$ vertices.
    26→
    27→This appears as Problem 1 in [EGT92]. The general behaviour of $\tau(G)$ is the subject of
    28→Problem 610.
    29→
    30→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/151](https://www.erdosproblems.com/151)
    31→
    32→[Er88] Erdős, P., _Problems and results in combinatorial analysis and graph theory_.
    33→Discrete Mathematics **72** (1988), 81-92.
    34→
    35→[EGT92] Erdős, P., Gallai, T. and Tuza, Zs., _Covering the cliques of a graph with vertices_.
    36→Discrete Mathematics **108** (1992), 279-289.
    37→-/
    38→
    39→open SimpleGraph
    40→
    41→namespace Erdos151
    42→
    43→/-- $S$ is a maximal clique of $G$ (represented as a `Finset`): it is a clique and
    44→no vertex outside $S$ can be added while preserving the clique property. -/
    45→def IsMaximalCliqueFS {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (S : Finset (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    46→  G.IsClique (S : Set (Fin n)) ∧
    47→  ∀ v : Fin n, v ∉ S → ¬G.IsClique (↑(insert v S) : Set (Fin n))
    48→
    49→/-- $T$ is a clique transversal of $G$ if $T$ has non-empty intersection with every
    50→maximal clique of $G$ that has at least $2$ vertices. -/
    51→def IsCliqueTransversal {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) (T : Finset (Fin n)) : Prop :=
    52→  ∀ S : Finset (Fin n), IsMaximalCliqueFS G S → 2 ≤ S.card → (T ∩ S).Nonempty
    53→
    54→/-- The clique transversal number $\tau(G)$: the minimum cardinality of a clique
    55→transversal of $G$. -/
    56→noncomputable def cliqueTransversalNumber {n : ℕ} (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) : ℕ :=
    57→  sInf { k : ℕ | ∃ T : Finset (Fin n), IsCliqueTransversal G T ∧ T.card = k }
    58→
    59→/-- $H(n)$ is maximal such that every triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices contains
    60→an independent set of size $H(n)$; equivalently, $H(n)$ is the minimum
    61→independence number over all triangle-free graphs on $n$ vertices. -/
    62→noncomputable def H (n : ℕ) : ℕ :=
    63→  sInf { k : ℕ | ∃ G : SimpleGraph (Fin n), G.CliqueFree 3 ∧ G.indepNum = k }
    64→
    65→/--
    66→Erdős Problem 151 [Er88, p.82] [EGT92, p.280] (problem of Erdős and Gallai):
    67→If $G$ is a graph on $n$ vertices then $\tau(G) \leq n - H(n)$,
    68→where $\tau(G)$ is the clique transversal number of $G$ and $H(n)$ is the maximum $k$
    69→such that every triangle-free graph on $n$ vertices contains an independent set
    70→of size $k$.
    71→-/
    72→@[category research open, AMS 5]
    73→theorem erdos_151 : answer(sorry) ↔
    74→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (G : SimpleGraph (Fin n)),
    75→      cliqueTransversalNumber G ≤ n - H n := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos151
    79→

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 163
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/163](https://www.erdosproblems.com/163)
    24→
    25→The Burr-Erdős conjecture: For any $d \geq 1$, if $H$ is a graph on $n$ vertices such
    26→that every subgraph of $H$ contains a vertex of degree at most $d$ (i.e., $H$ is
    27→$d$-degenerate), then $R(H) \leq C_d \cdot n$ for some constant $C_d$ depending only on $d$.
    28→
    29→Proved by Lee [Le17], who showed $R(H) \leq 2^{2^{O(d)}} \cdot n$.
    30→
    31→[BuEr75] Burr, S. A. and Erdős, P., _On the magnitude of generalized Ramsey numbers for graphs_.
    32→Infinite and finite sets, Vol. 1 (1975), 214–240.
    33→
    34→[Er82e] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on finite and infinite graphs_. Recent advances
    35→in graph theory (Proc. Second Czechoslovak Sympos., Prague, 1982).
    36→
    37→[Le17] Lee, C., _Ramsey numbers of degenerate graphs_. Ann. of Math. **186** (2017), 791–829.
    38→
    39→See also Problem 800, which is a special case of this conjecture.
    40→-/
    41→
    42→open SimpleGraph Finset
    43→
    44→namespace Erdos163
    45→
    46→/-- A simple graph $H$ on a finite vertex type is $d$-degenerate if every nonempty
    47→    subset $S$ of vertices contains a vertex $v$ with at most $d$ neighbours in $S$. -/
    48→def IsDDegenerate {V : Type*} [Fintype V] [DecidableEq V]
    49→    (H : SimpleGraph V) [DecidableRel H.Adj] (d : ℕ) : Prop :=
    50→  ∀ S : Finset V, S.Nonempty →
    51→    ∃ v ∈ S, (S.filter (H.Adj v)).card ≤ d
    52→
    53→/-- The diagonal Ramsey number $R(H)$: the minimum $N$ such that for every simple
    54→    graph $G$ on $\operatorname{Fin} N$, either $G$ or $G^c$ contains a copy of $H$. -/
    55→noncomputable def ramseyDiag {U : Type*} (H : SimpleGraph U) : ℕ :=
    56→  sInf {N : ℕ | ∀ (G : SimpleGraph (Fin N)),
    57→    H.IsContained G ∨ H.IsContained Gᶜ}
    58→
    59→/--
    60→Erdős Problem 163 [BuEr75, Er82e] — The Burr-Erdős Conjecture:
    61→
    62→For every $d \geq 1$, there exists a constant $C_d > 0$ such that for every
    63→$d$-degenerate graph $H$ on $n$ vertices, $R(H, H) \leq C_d \cdot n$.
    64→
    65→Equivalently, if $H$ is the union of $c$ forests then $R(H) \leq C_c \cdot n$.
    66→
    67→Proved by Lee [Le17], who showed $R(H) \leq 2^{2^{O(d)}} \cdot n$.
    68→-/
    69→@[category research solved, AMS 5]
    70→theorem erdos_163 :
    71→    ∀ d : ℕ, 1 ≤ d →
    72→    ∃ C : ℝ, 0 < C ∧
    73→    ∀ (n : ℕ) (H : SimpleGraph (Fin n)) [DecidableRel H.Adj],
    74→    IsDDegenerate H d →
    75→      (ramseyDiag H : ℝ) ≤ C * (n : ℝ) := by
    76→  sorry
    77→
    78→end Erdos163

    20→/-!
    21→# Erdős Problem 154
    22→
    23→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/154](https://www.erdosproblems.com/154)
    24→
    25→Let $A \subset \{1, \ldots, N\}$ be a Sidon set with $|A| \sim N^{1/2}$.
    26→Must the sumset $A + A$ be well-distributed over all small moduli?
    27→
    28→[ESS94] Erdős, P. and Sárközy, A. and Sós, V. T., _On Sum Sets of Sidon Sets, I_.
    29→  Journal of Number Theory (1994), 329–347.
    30→
    31→[Li98] Lindström, B., _Well distribution of Sidon sets in residue classes_.
    32→  J. Number Theory (1998), 197–200.
    33→
    34→[Ko99] Kolountzakis, M. N., _On the uniform distribution in residue classes of dense sets
    35→  of integers with distinct sums_. J. Number Theory (1999), 147–153.
    36→-/
    37→
    38→open Filter
    39→
    40→open scoped Topology Real Pointwise
    41→
    42→namespace Erdos154
    43→
    44→/-- The fraction of elements in a finite set of naturals that are congruent to $r$ modulo $m$. -/
    45→noncomputable def modFraction (m r : ℕ) (S : Finset ℕ) : ℝ :=
    46→  ((S.filter (fun n => n % m = r)).card : ℝ) / (S.card : ℝ)
    47→
    48→/--
    49→Erdős Problem \#154 [ESS94]:
    50→
    51→Let $A \subset \{1, \ldots, N\}$ be a Sidon set with $|A| \sim N^{1/2}$. Must $A + A$ be
    52→well-distributed over all small moduli? In particular, must about half
    53→the elements of $A + A$ be even and half odd?
    54→
    55→Proved in the affirmative. Lindström [Li98] showed that $A$ itself is
    56→well-distributed modulo small integers (e.g. $|A \cap \{\text{evens}\}| \approx |A|/2$),
    57→subsequently strengthened by Kolountzakis [Ko99]. The extension to $A + A$
    58→follows immediately from the Sidon property: if $A$ has $e$ even and $o$ odd
    59→elements, then $A + A$ has exactly $e(e+1)/2 + o(o+1)/2$ even elements
    60→and $eo$ odd elements (all distinct by the Sidon property), and the
    61→distribution is approximately $1/2$ each when $e \approx o \approx |A|/2$.
    62→
    63→Formalized as: for any sequence $(A_n)_n$ of Sidon sets $A_n \subset \{0, \ldots, n\}$
    64→with $|A_n| / \sqrt{n} \to 1$ as $n \to \infty$, and any fixed modulus $m \geq 1$ and
    65→residue $0 \leq r < m$, the fraction of elements of $A_n + A_n$ in residue
    66→class $r \bmod m$ tends to $1/m$. Here $A_n + A_n$ denotes the pointwise sumset.
    67→-/
    68→@[category research solved, AMS 5 11]
    69→theorem erdos_154 : answer(True) ↔
    70→    ∀ (A : ℕ → Finset ℕ),
    71→      (∀ n, IsSidon (↑(A n) : Set ℕ)) →
    72→      (∀ n, (A n) ⊆ Finset.range (n + 1)) →
    73→      Tendsto (fun n => ((A n).card : ℝ) / Real.sqrt n) atTop (𝓝 1) →
    74→      ∀ (m : ℕ), 1 ≤ m →
    75→        ∀ r < m,
    76→          Tendsto (fun n => modFraction m r (A n + A n)) atTop (𝓝 (1 / (m : ℝ))) := by
    77→  sorry
    78→
    79→end Erdos154

1→/-
     2→Copyright (c) 2021 Matt Kempster. All rights reserved.
     3→Released under Apache 2.0 license as described in the file LICENSE.
     4→Authors: Matt Kempster
     5→-/
     6→import Mathlib.Geometry.Euclidean.Triangle
     7→
     8→/-!
     9→# Freek № 57: Heron's Formula
    10→
    11→This file proves Theorem 57 from the [100 Theorems List](https://www.cs.ru.nl/~freek/100/),
    12→also known as Heron's formula, which gives the area of a triangle based only on its three sides'
    13→lengths.
    14→
    15→## References
    16→
    17→* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Herons_formula
    18→
    19→-/
    20→
    21→
    22→open Real EuclideanGeometry
    23→
    24→open scoped Real EuclideanGeometry
    25→
    26→namespace Theorems100
    27→
    28→local notation "√" => Real.sqrt
    29→
    30→variable {V : Type*} {P : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V] [MetricSpace P]
    31→  [NormedAddTorsor V P]
    32→
    33→/-- **Heron's formula**: The area of a triangle with side lengths `a`, `b`, and `c` is
    34→  `√(s * (s - a) * (s - b) * (s - c))` where `s = (a + b + c) / 2` is the semiperimeter.
    35→  We show this by equating this formula to `a * b * sin γ`, where `γ` is the angle opposite
    36→  the side `c`.
    37→-/
    38→theorem heron {p₁ p₂ p₃ : P} (h1 : p₁ ≠ p₂) (h2 : p₃ ≠ p₂) :
    39→    let a := dist p₁ p₂
    40→    let b := dist p₃ p₂
    41→    let c := dist p₁ p₃
    42→    let s := (a + b + c) / 2
    43→    1 / 2 * a * b * sin (∠ p₁ p₂ p₃) = √(s * (s - a) * (s - b) * (s - c)) := by
    44→  intro a b c s
    45→  let γ := ∠ p₁ p₂ p₃
    46→  obtain := (dist_pos.mpr h1).ne', (dist_pos.mpr h2).ne'
    47→  have cos_rule : cos γ = (a * a + b * b - c * c) / (2 * a * b) := by
    48→    simp [field, a, b, c, γ, mul_comm,
    49→      dist_sq_eq_dist_sq_add_dist_sq_sub_two_mul_dist_mul_dist_mul_cos_angle p₁ p₂ p₃]
    50→  let numerator := (2 * a * b) ^ 2 - (a * a + b * b - c * c) ^ 2
    51→  let denominator := (2 * a * b) ^ 2
    52→  have split_to_frac : ↑1 - cos γ ^ 2 = numerator / denominator := by
    53→    simp [field, numerator, denominator, cos_rule]
    54→  have numerator_nonneg : 0 ≤ numerator := by
    55→    have frac_nonneg : 0 ≤ numerator / denominator :=
    56→      (sub_nonneg.mpr (cos_sq_le_one γ)).trans_eq split_to_frac
    57→    rcases div_nonneg_iff.mp frac_nonneg with h | h
    58→    · exact h.left
    59→    · simpa [numerator, denominator, a, b, c, h1, h2] using le_antisymm h.right (sq_nonneg _)
    60→  have ab2_nonneg : 0 ≤ 2 * a * b := by positivity
    61→  calc
    62→    1 / 2 * a * b * sin γ = 1 / 2 * a * b * (√numerator / √denominator) := by
    63→      rw [sin_eq_sqrt_one_sub_cos_sq, split_to_frac, sqrt_div numerator_nonneg] <;>
    64→        simp [γ, angle_nonneg, angle_le_pi]
    65→    _ = 1 / 4 * √((2 * a * b) ^ 2 - (a * a + b * b - c * c) ^ 2) := by
    66→      simp (disch := positivity) [field, numerator, denominator, -mul_eq_mul_left_iff]; ring
    67→    _ = ↑1 / ↑4 * √(s * (s - a) * (s - b) * (s - c) * ↑4 ^ 2) := by simp only [s]; ring_nf
    68→    _ = √(s * (s - a) * (s - b) * (s - c)) := by
    69→      rw [sqrt_mul', sqrt_sq, div_mul_eq_mul_div, one_mul, mul_div_cancel_right₀] <;> norm_num
    70→
    71→end Theorems100
    72→

    14→This file proves basic geometric results about distances and angles in (possibly degenerate)
    15→right-angled triangles in real inner product spaces and Euclidean affine spaces.
    16→
    17→## Implementation notes
    18→
    19→Results in this file are generally given in a form with only those non-degeneracy conditions
    20→needed for the particular result, rather than requiring affine independence of the points of a
    21→triangle unnecessarily.
    22→
    23→## References
    24→
    25→* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pythagorean_theorem
    26→
    27→-/
    28→
    29→public section
    30→
    31→
    32→noncomputable section
    33→
    34→open scoped EuclideanGeometry
    35→
    36→open scoped Real
    37→
    38→open scoped RealInnerProductSpace
    39→
    40→namespace InnerProductGeometry
    41→
    42→variable {V : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V]
    43→
    44→/-- Pythagorean theorem, if-and-only-if vector angle form. -/
    45→theorem norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_iff_angle_eq_pi_div_two (x y : V) :
    46→    ‖x + y‖ * ‖x + y‖ = ‖x‖ * ‖x‖ + ‖y‖ * ‖y‖ ↔ angle x y = π / 2 := by
    47→  rw [norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_iff_real_inner_eq_zero]
    48→  exact inner_eq_zero_iff_angle_eq_pi_div_two x y
    49→
    50→/-- Pythagorean theorem, vector angle form. -/
    51→theorem norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq' (x y : V) (h : angle x y = π / 2) :
    52→    ‖x + y‖ * ‖x + y‖ = ‖x‖ * ‖x‖ + ‖y‖ * ‖y‖ :=
    53→  (norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_iff_angle_eq_pi_div_two x y).2 h
    54→
    55→/-- Pythagorean theorem, subtracting vectors, if-and-only-if vector angle form. -/
    56→theorem norm_sub_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_iff_angle_eq_pi_div_two (x y : V) :
    57→    ‖x - y‖ * ‖x - y‖ = ‖x‖ * ‖x‖ + ‖y‖ * ‖y‖ ↔ angle x y = π / 2 := by
    58→  rw [norm_sub_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_iff_real_inner_eq_zero]
    59→  exact inner_eq_zero_iff_angle_eq_pi_div_two x y
    60→
    61→/-- Pythagorean theorem, subtracting vectors, vector angle form. -/
    62→theorem norm_sub_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq' (x y : V) (h : angle x y = π / 2) :
    63→    ‖x - y‖ * ‖x - y‖ = ‖x‖ * ‖x‖ + ‖y‖ * ‖y‖ :=
    64→  (norm_sub_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_iff_angle_eq_pi_div_two x y).2 h
    65→
    66→/-- An angle in a right-angled triangle expressed using `arccos`. -/
    67→theorem angle_add_eq_arccos_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) :
    68→    angle x (x + y) = Real.arccos (‖x‖ / ‖x + y‖) := by
    69→  rw [angle, inner_add_right, h, add_zero, real_inner_self_eq_norm_mul_norm]
    70→  by_cases hx : ‖x‖ = 0; · simp [hx]
    71→  rw [div_mul_eq_div_div, mul_self_div_self]
    72→
    73→/-- An angle in a right-angled triangle expressed using `arcsin`. -/
    74→theorem angle_add_eq_arcsin_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) (h0 : x ≠ 0 ∨ y ≠ 0) :
    75→    angle x (x + y) = Real.arcsin (‖y‖ / ‖x + y‖) := by
    76→  have hxy : ‖x + y‖ ^ 2 ≠ 0 := by
    77→    rw [pow_two, norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_real h, ne_comm]
    78→    refine ne_of_lt ?_
    79→    rcases h0 with (h0 | h0)
    80→    · exact
    81→        Left.add_pos_of_pos_of_nonneg (mul_self_pos.2 (norm_ne_zero_iff.2 h0)) (mul_self_nonneg _)
    82→    · exact
    83→        Left.add_pos_of_nonneg_of_pos (mul_self_nonneg _) (mul_self_pos.2 (norm_ne_zero_iff.2 h0))
    84→  rw [angle_add_eq_arccos_of_inner_eq_zero h,
    85→    Real.arccos_eq_arcsin (div_nonneg (norm_nonneg _) (norm_nonneg _)), div_pow, one_sub_div hxy]
    86→  nth_rw 1 [pow_two]
    87→  rw [norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_real h, pow_two, add_sub_cancel_left, ← pow_two, ← div_pow,
    88→    Real.sqrt_sq (div_nonneg (norm_nonneg _) (norm_nonneg _))]
    89→
    90→/-- An angle in a right-angled triangle expressed using `arctan`. -/
    91→theorem angle_add_eq_arctan_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) (h0 : x ≠ 0) :
    92→    angle x (x + y) = Real.arctan (‖y‖ / ‖x‖) := by
    93→  rw [angle_add_eq_arcsin_of_inner_eq_zero h (Or.inl h0), Real.arctan_eq_arcsin, ←
    94→    div_mul_eq_div_div, norm_add_eq_sqrt_iff_real_inner_eq_zero.2 h]
    95→  nth_rw 3 [← Real.sqrt_sq (norm_nonneg x)]
    96→  rw_mod_cast [← Real.sqrt_mul (sq_nonneg _), div_pow, pow_two, pow_two, mul_add, mul_one, mul_div,
    97→    mul_comm (‖x‖ * ‖x‖), ← mul_div, div_self (mul_self_pos.2 (norm_ne_zero_iff.2 h0)).ne', mul_one]
    98→
    99→/-- An angle in a non-degenerate right-angled triangle is positive. -/
   100→theorem angle_add_pos_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) (h0 : x = 0 ∨ y ≠ 0) :
   101→    0 < angle x (x + y) := by
   102→  rw [angle_add_eq_arccos_of_inner_eq_zero h, Real.arccos_pos,
   103→    norm_add_eq_sqrt_iff_real_inner_eq_zero.2 h]
   104→  by_cases hx : x = 0; · simp [hx]
   105→  rw [div_lt_one (Real.sqrt_pos.2 (Left.add_pos_of_pos_of_nonneg (mul_self_pos.2
   106→    (norm_ne_zero_iff.2 hx)) (mul_self_nonneg _))), Real.lt_sqrt (norm_nonneg _), pow_two]
   107→  simpa [hx] using h0
   108→
   109→/-- An angle in a right-angled triangle is at most `π / 2`. -/
   110→theorem angle_add_le_pi_div_two_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) :
   111→    angle x (x + y) ≤ π / 2 := by
   112→  rw [angle_add_eq_arccos_of_inner_eq_zero h, Real.arccos_le_pi_div_two]
   113→  exact div_nonneg (norm_nonneg _) (norm_nonneg _)
   114→
   115→/-- An angle in a non-degenerate right-angled triangle is less than `π / 2`. -/
   116→theorem angle_add_lt_pi_div_two_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) (h0 : x ≠ 0) :
   117→    angle x (x + y) < π / 2 := by
   118→  rw [angle_add_eq_arccos_of_inner_eq_zero h, Real.arccos_lt_pi_div_two,
   119→    norm_add_eq_sqrt_iff_real_inner_eq_zero.2 h]
   120→  exact div_pos (norm_pos_iff.2 h0) (Real.sqrt_pos.2 (Left.add_pos_of_pos_of_nonneg
   121→    (mul_self_pos.2 (norm_ne_zero_iff.2 h0)) (mul_self_nonneg _)))
   122→
   123→/-- The cosine of an angle in a right-angled triangle as a ratio of sides. -/
   124→theorem cos_angle_add_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) :
   125→    Real.cos (angle x (x + y)) = ‖x‖ / ‖x + y‖ := by
   126→  rw [angle_add_eq_arccos_of_inner_eq_zero h,
   127→    Real.cos_arccos (le_trans (by simp) (div_nonneg (norm_nonneg _) (norm_nonneg _)))
   128→      (div_le_one_of_le₀ _ (norm_nonneg _))]
   129→  rw [mul_self_le_mul_self_iff (norm_nonneg _) (norm_nonneg _),
   130→    norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_real h]
   131→  exact le_add_of_nonneg_right (mul_self_nonneg _)
   132→
   133→/-- The sine of an angle in a right-angled triangle as a ratio of sides. -/
   134→theorem sin_angle_add_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) (h0 : x ≠ 0 ∨ y ≠ 0) :
   135→    Real.sin (angle x (x + y)) = ‖y‖ / ‖x + y‖ := by
   136→  rw [angle_add_eq_arcsin_of_inner_eq_zero h h0,
   137→    Real.sin_arcsin (le_trans (by simp) (div_nonneg (norm_nonneg _) (norm_nonneg _)))
   138→      (div_le_one_of_le₀ _ (norm_nonneg _))]
   139→  rw [mul_self_le_mul_self_iff (norm_nonneg _) (norm_nonneg _),
   140→    norm_add_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_real h]
   141→  exact le_add_of_nonneg_left (mul_self_nonneg _)
   142→
   143→/-- The tangent of an angle in a right-angled triangle as a ratio of sides. -/
   144→theorem tan_angle_add_of_inner_eq_zero {x y : V} (h : ⟪x, y⟫ = 0) :
   145→    Real.tan (angle x (x + y)) = ‖y‖ / ‖x‖ := by
   146→  by_cases h0 : x = 0; · simp [h0]
   147→  rw [angle_add_eq_arctan_of_inner_eq_zero h h0, Real.tan_arctan]
   148→
   149→/-- The cosine of an angle in a right-angled triangle multiplied by the hypotenuse equals the
   150→adjacent side. -/

    13→See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    14→limitations under the License.
    15→-/
    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 353
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/353](https://www.erdosproblems.com/353)
    23→
    24→Erdős asked whether every measurable subset of $\mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite Lebesgue measure
    25→must contain the vertices of an isosceles trapezoid of area $1$, and similarly for isosceles
    26→triangles, right-angled triangles, and cyclic quadrilaterals of area $1$.
    27→
    28→[Er83d] Erdős, P., _Problems and results on combinatorial geometry_.
    29→
    30→[Ko25] Koizumi, S., _On geometric configurations in measurable sets of infinite measure_.
    31→
    32→[KoPr24] Kovač, V. and Predojević, B., _Cyclic quadrilaterals in measurable sets_.
    33→-/
    34→
    35→open MeasureTheory
    36→
    37→namespace Erdos353
    38→
    39→/-- Squared Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    40→noncomputable def sqDist (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    41→  (p.1 - q.1) ^ 2 + (p.2 - q.2) ^ 2
    42→
    43→/-- Area of a triangle with vertices $p_1$, $p_2$, $p_3$ via the cross product formula. -/
    44→noncomputable def triangleArea (p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    45→  |(p₂.1 - p₁.1) * (p₃.2 - p₁.2) - (p₃.1 - p₁.1) * (p₂.2 - p₁.2)| / 2
    46→
    47→/-- Area of a quadrilateral with consecutive vertices $p_1$, $p_2$, $p_3$, $p_4$ via the
    48→shoelace formula. -/
    49→noncomputable def quadArea (p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    50→  |(p₁.1 * p₂.2 - p₂.1 * p₁.2 + p₂.1 * p₃.2 - p₃.1 * p₂.2 +
    51→    p₃.1 * p₄.2 - p₄.1 * p₃.2 + p₄.1 * p₁.2 - p₁.1 * p₄.2)| / 2
    52→
    53→/-- Two vectors in $\mathbb{R}^2$ are parallel (cross product is zero). -/
    54→def Parallel (v w : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    55→  v.1 * w.2 = v.2 * w.1
    56→
    57→/-- Three points form an isosceles triangle: all distinct with at least two sides
    58→of equal length. -/
    59→def IsIsoscelesTriangle (p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    60→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧
    61→  (sqDist p₁ p₂ = sqDist p₁ p₃ ∨
    62→   sqDist p₁ p₂ = sqDist p₂ p₃ ∨
    63→   sqDist p₁ p₃ = sqDist p₂ p₃)
    64→
    65→/-- Three points form a right-angled triangle: all distinct with one angle equal
    66→to $90°$ (dot product of adjacent edges is zero at some vertex). -/
    67→def IsRightTriangle (p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    68→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧
    69→  ((p₂.1 - p₁.1) * (p₃.1 - p₁.1) + (p₂.2 - p₁.2) * (p₃.2 - p₁.2) = 0 ∨
    70→   (p₁.1 - p₂.1) * (p₃.1 - p₂.1) + (p₁.2 - p₂.2) * (p₃.2 - p₂.2) = 0 ∨
    71→   (p₁.1 - p₃.1) * (p₂.1 - p₃.1) + (p₁.2 - p₃.2) * (p₂.2 - p₃.2) = 0)
    72→
    73→/-- Four points (in order) form an isosceles trapezoid: exactly one pair of
    74→parallel opposite sides, with the non-parallel sides (legs) of equal length.
    75→Here $p_1 p_2 \parallel p_4 p_3$ are the parallel sides and $p_1 p_4$, $p_2 p_3$
    76→are the equal legs. -/
    77→def IsIsoscelesTrapezoid (p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    78→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₃ ≠ p₄ ∧
    79→  Parallel (p₂.1 - p₁.1, p₂.2 - p₁.2) (p₃.1 - p₄.1, p₃.2 - p₄.2) ∧
    80→  sqDist p₁ p₄ = sqDist p₂ p₃ ∧
    81→  ¬ Parallel (p₄.1 - p₁.1, p₄.2 - p₁.2) (p₃.1 - p₂.1, p₃.2 - p₂.2)
    82→
    83→/-- Four distinct points lie on a common circle. -/
    84→def IsCyclicQuadrilateral (p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    85→  p₁ ≠ p₂ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₁ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₃ ∧ p₂ ≠ p₄ ∧ p₃ ≠ p₄ ∧
    86→  ∃ (c : ℝ × ℝ) (r_sq : ℝ), 0 < r_sq ∧
    87→    sqDist c p₁ = r_sq ∧ sqDist c p₂ = r_sq ∧
    88→    sqDist c p₃ = r_sq ∧ sqDist c p₄ = r_sq
    89→
    90→/--
    91→Erdős Problem 353, Part 1 [Er83d]:
    92→
    93→Let $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ be a measurable set with infinite Lebesgue measure. Must $A$
    94→contain the vertices of an isosceles trapezoid of area $1$?
    95→
    96→Proved by Koizumi [Ko25].
    97→-/
    98→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
    99→theorem erdos_353 : answer(True) ↔
   100→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   101→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ,
   102→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧ p₄ ∈ A ∧
   103→      IsIsoscelesTrapezoid p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ ∧
   104→      quadArea p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ = 1 := by
   105→  sorry
   106→
   107→/--
   108→Erdős Problem 353, Part 2 [Er83d]:
   109→
   110→Must a measurable set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite measure contain the vertices of
   111→an isosceles triangle of area $1$?
   112→
   113→Proved by Koizumi [Ko25].
   114→-/
   115→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
   116→theorem erdos_353.variants.isosceles_triangle : answer(True) ↔
   117→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   118→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ,
   119→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧
   120→      IsIsoscelesTriangle p₁ p₂ p₃ ∧
   121→      triangleArea p₁ p₂ p₃ = 1 := by
   122→  sorry
   123→
   124→/--
   125→Erdős Problem 353, Part 3 [Er83d]:
   126→
   127→Must a measurable set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite measure contain the vertices of
   128→a right-angled triangle of area $1$?
   129→
   130→Proved by Koizumi [Ko25].
   131→-/
   132→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
   133→theorem erdos_353.variants.right_triangle : answer(True) ↔
   134→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   135→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ : ℝ × ℝ,
   136→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧
   137→      IsRightTriangle p₁ p₂ p₃ ∧
   138→      triangleArea p₁ p₂ p₃ = 1 := by
   139→  sorry
   140→
   141→/--
   142→Erdős Problem 353, Part 4 [Er83d]:
   143→
   144→Must a measurable set $A \subseteq \mathbb{R}^2$ with infinite measure contain four distinct
   145→points on a common circle forming a quadrilateral of area $1$?
   146→
   147→Proved by Kovač and Predojević [KoPr24].
   148→-/
   149→@[category research solved, AMS 28 52]
   150→theorem erdos_353.variants.cyclic_quadrilateral : answer(True) ↔
   151→    ∀ (A : Set (ℝ × ℝ)), MeasurableSet A → volume A = ⊤ →
   152→    ∃ p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ : ℝ × ℝ,
   153→      p₁ ∈ A ∧ p₂ ∈ A ∧ p₃ ∈ A ∧ p₄ ∈ A ∧
   154→      IsCyclicQuadrilateral p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ ∧
   155→      quadArea p₁ p₂ p₃ p₄ = 1 := by
   156→  sorry
   157→
   158→end Erdos353

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 633
    21→
    22→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/633](https://www.erdosproblems.com/633)
    23→
    24→Classify those triangles which can only be cut into a square number of congruent
    25→triangles.
    26→
    27→Any triangle can be cut into $n^2$ congruent triangles for any $n \geq 1$. Soifer [So09]
    28→proved that there exists at least one triangle (e.g. one with sides $\sqrt{2}$, $\sqrt{3}$,
    29→$\sqrt{4}$) which can only be cut into a square number of congruent triangles. In fact,
    30→Soifer proved that any triangle for which the angles and sides are both
    31→integrally independent has this property. The full classification remains open.
    32→
    33→[So09] Soifer, A., *The Mathematical Coloring Book*, Springer, 2009.
    34→-/
    35→
    36→namespace Erdos633
    37→
    38→/-- Squared Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    39→noncomputable def sqDist (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    40→  (p.1 - q.1) ^ 2 + (p.2 - q.2) ^ 2
    41→
    42→/-- Multiset of squared side lengths of a triangle.
    43→Two triangles are congruent (by SSS) iff these multisets agree. -/
    44→noncomputable def sideLengthsSq (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Multiset ℝ :=
    45→  ↑[sqDist A B, sqDist B C, sqDist A C]
    46→
    47→/-- A triangle is non-degenerate (vertices not collinear). -/
    48→def NonDegenerate (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    49→  (B.1 - A.1) * (C.2 - A.2) - (C.1 - A.1) * (B.2 - A.2) ≠ 0
    50→
    51→/-- The closed triangular region: convex hull of vertices $A$, $B$, $C$.
    52→A point $p$ lies in the triangle iff $p = \alpha A + \beta B + \gamma C$ for some
    53→$\alpha, \beta, \gamma \geq 0$ with $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = 1$. -/
    54→def triangleRegion (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    55→  {p | ∃ (α β γ : ℝ), 0 ≤ α ∧ 0 ≤ β ∧ 0 ≤ γ ∧ α + β + γ = 1 ∧
    56→    p.1 = α * A.1 + β * B.1 + γ * C.1 ∧
    57→    p.2 = α * A.2 + β * B.2 + γ * C.2}
    58→
    59→/-- The open interior of a triangular region: strictly positive
    60→barycentric coordinates. -/
    61→def triangleInterior (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Set (ℝ × ℝ) :=
    62→  {p | ∃ (α β γ : ℝ), 0 < α ∧ 0 < β ∧ 0 < γ ∧ α + β + γ = 1 ∧
    63→    p.1 = α * A.1 + β * B.1 + γ * C.1 ∧
    64→    p.2 = α * A.2 + β * B.2 + γ * C.2}
    65→
    66→/-- Triangle $(A, B, C)$ can be dissected into exactly $n$ pairwise-congruent
    67→non-degenerate triangles that tile it: each is contained in the original,
    68→their interiors are pairwise disjoint, and their union covers the original. -/
    69→def CanDissectInto (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=
    70→  ∃ (V : Fin n → (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ)),
    71→    -- All sub-triangles are non-degenerate
    72→    (∀ i, NonDegenerate (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2) ∧
    73→    -- All sub-triangles are mutually congruent (same squared side lengths)
    74→    (∀ i j, sideLengthsSq (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 =
    75→            sideLengthsSq (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2) ∧
    76→    -- Each sub-triangle is contained in the original
    77→    (∀ i, triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ⊆
    78→           triangleRegion A B C) ∧
    79→    -- Pairwise disjoint interiors
    80→    (∀ i j, i ≠ j →
    81→      triangleInterior (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2 ∩
    82→      triangleInterior (V j).1 (V j).2.1 (V j).2.2 = ∅) ∧
    83→    -- Union covers the original triangle
    84→    (∀ p, p ∈ triangleRegion A B C →
    85→      ∃ i, p ∈ triangleRegion (V i).1 (V i).2.1 (V i).2.2)
    86→
    87→/-- Euclidean distance (side length) between two points. -/
    88→noncomputable def sideLength (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    89→  Real.sqrt (sqDist p q)
    90→
    91→/-- The sides of a triangle are integrally independent: no nontrivial integer
    92→linear combination of the three side lengths equals zero. -/
    93→def IntIndepSides (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    94→  ∀ (a₁ a₂ a₃ : ℤ),
    95→    ↑a₁ * sideLength B C + ↑a₂ * sideLength A C +
    96→      ↑a₃ * sideLength A B = 0 →
    97→    a₁ = 0 ∧ a₂ = 0 ∧ a₃ = 0
    98→
    99→/-- The angle at vertex $P$ in triangle $PQR$, via the dot-product formula:
   100→$\arccos\bigl(\frac{\vec{PQ} \cdot \vec{PR}}{|PQ| \cdot |PR|}\bigr)$.
   101→Returns a value in $[0, \pi]$. -/
   102→noncomputable def angle (P Q R : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
   103→  Real.arccos (((Q.1 - P.1) * (R.1 - P.1) + (Q.2 - P.2) * (R.2 - P.2)) /
   104→    (sideLength P Q * sideLength P R))
   105→
   106→/-- The angles of a triangle are integrally independent: the only integer
   107→linear combination of the angles that equals an integer multiple of $\pi$
   108→is the trivial one (all coefficients equal). This accounts for the
   109→constraint $\alpha + \beta + \gamma = \pi$. -/
   110→def IntIndepAngles (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
   111→  ∀ (a₁ a₂ a₃ k : ℤ),
   112→    ↑a₁ * angle A B C + ↑a₂ * angle B A C +
   113→      ↑a₃ * angle C A B = ↑k * Real.pi →
   114→    a₁ = a₂ ∧ a₂ = a₃
   115→
   116→/--
   117→Every non-degenerate triangle can be dissected into $n^2$ congruent triangles
   118→for any positive integer $n$.
   119→-/
   120→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   121→theorem erdos_633.variants.square_dissection
   122→    (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   123→    (n : ℕ) (hn : 1 ≤ n) :
   124→    CanDissectInto A B C (n ^ 2) := by
   125→  sorry
   126→
   127→/--
   128→There exists a non-degenerate triangle which can be dissected into $n$
   129→congruent triangles only when $n$ is a perfect square. [So09]
   130→-/
   131→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   132→theorem erdos_633.variants.existence :
   133→    ∃ A B C : ℝ × ℝ, NonDegenerate A B C ∧
   134→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto A B C n → ∃ m : ℕ, n = m ^ 2 := by
   135→  sorry
   136→
   137→/--
   138→If a non-degenerate triangle has integrally independent sides and angles,
   139→then it can only be dissected into a perfect square number of congruent
   140→triangles. [So09]
   141→-/
   142→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
   143→theorem erdos_633.variants.soifer
   144→    (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (hnd : NonDegenerate A B C)
   145→    (hsides : IntIndepSides A B C) (hangles : IntIndepAngles A B C) :
   146→    ∀ n : ℕ, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto A B C n → ∃ m : ℕ, n = m ^ 2 := by
   147→  sorry
   148→
   149→/--
   150→Classify those non-degenerate triangles that can only be cut into a perfect
   151→square number of congruent triangles. The full classification remains open.
   152→-/
   153→@[category research open, AMS 51]
   154→theorem erdos_633 :
   155→    {T : (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) × (ℝ × ℝ) |
   156→      NonDegenerate T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 ∧
   157→      ∀ n, 1 ≤ n → CanDissectInto T.1 T.2.1 T.2.2 n → ∃ m, n = m ^ 2} =
   158→    answer(sorry) := by sorry
   159→
   160→end Erdos633
   161→

    63→def CanDissectInto (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) (n : ℕ) : Prop :=

    16→
    17→import FormalConjectures.Util.ProblemImports
    18→
    19→/-!
    20→# Erdős Problem 898
    21→
    22→If P is a point in the interior of triangle ABC, and D_a, D_b, D_c are the feet of the
    23→perpendiculars from P to the sides BC, CA, AB respectively, then
    24→PA + PB + PC ≥ 2(PD_a + PD_b + PD_c).
    25→
    26→*Reference:* [erdosproblems.com/898](https://www.erdosproblems.com/898)
    27→
    28→[Er82e] Erdős, P., *Personal favorites*, p.61.
    29→-/
    30→
    31→namespace Erdos898
    32→
    33→/-- Squared Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    34→noncomputable def sqDist898 (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    35→  (p.1 - q.1) ^ 2 + (p.2 - q.2) ^ 2
    36→
    37→/-- Euclidean distance between two points in $\mathbb{R}^2$. -/
    38→noncomputable def dist898 (p q : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ :=
    39→  Real.sqrt (sqDist898 p q)
    40→
    41→/-- A triangle is non-degenerate (vertices not collinear). -/
    42→def NonDegenerate898 (A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    43→  (B.1 - A.1) * (C.2 - A.2) - (C.1 - A.1) * (B.2 - A.2) ≠ 0
    44→
    45→/-- Point $P$ lies in the open interior of triangle $ABC$
    46→(strictly positive barycentric coordinates). -/
    47→def InInterior898 (P A B C : ℝ × ℝ) : Prop :=
    48→  ∃ (α β γ : ℝ), 0 < α ∧ 0 < β ∧ 0 < γ ∧ α + β + γ = 1 ∧
    49→    P.1 = α * A.1 + β * B.1 + γ * C.1 ∧
    50→    P.2 = α * A.2 + β * B.2 + γ * C.2
    51→
    52→/-- The foot of the perpendicular from point $P$ to the line through $A$ and $B$.
    53→This is the orthogonal projection of $P$ onto line $AB$:
    54→$$\text{foot} = A + t \cdot (B - A)$$
    55→where $t = \frac{(P - A) \cdot (B - A)}{|B - A|^2}$. -/
    56→noncomputable def perpFoot898 (P A B : ℝ × ℝ) : ℝ × ℝ :=
    57→  let dx := B.1 - A.1
    58→  let dy := B.2 - A.2
    59→  let t := ((P.1 - A.1) * dx + (P.2 - A.2) * dy) / (dx ^ 2 + dy ^ 2)
    60→  (A.1 + t * dx, A.2 + t * dy)
    61→
    62→/--
    63→Erdős Problem 898 (Erdős-Mordell Inequality) [Er82e, p.61]:
    64→
    65→If $A$, $B$, $C$ form a non-degenerate triangle in $\mathbb{R}^2$ and $P$ is a point in its
    66→interior, then
    67→$$
    68→  d(P, A) + d(P, B) + d(P, C) \geq 2 \cdot (d(P, D_a) + d(P, D_b) + d(P, D_c))
    69→$$
    70→where $D_a$, $D_b$, $D_c$ are the feet of the perpendiculars from $P$ to sides
    71→$BC$, $CA$, $AB$ respectively.
    72→
    73→Conjectured by Erdős in 1932 and proved by Mordell soon afterwards.
    74→-/
    75→@[category research solved, AMS 51]
    76→theorem erdos_898
    77→    (A B C P : ℝ × ℝ)
    78→    (hnd : NonDegenerate898 A B C)
    79→    (hP : InInterior898 P A B C) :
    80→    dist898 P A + dist898 P B + dist898 P C ≥
    81→      2 * (dist898 P (perpFoot898 P B C) +
    82→           dist898 P (perpFoot898 P A C) +
    83→           dist898 P (perpFoot898 P A B)) := by
    84→  sorry
    85→
    86→end Erdos898
    87→

    13→/-!
    14→# Triangles
    15→
    16→This file proves basic geometrical results about distances and angles
    17→in (possibly degenerate) triangles in real inner product spaces and
    18→Euclidean affine spaces. More specialized results, and results
    19→developed for simplices in general rather than just for triangles, are
    20→in separate files. Definitions and results that make sense in more
    21→general affine spaces rather than just in the Euclidean case go under
    22→`LinearAlgebra.AffineSpace`.
    23→
    24→## Implementation notes
    25→
    26→Results in this file are generally given in a form with only those
    27→non-degeneracy conditions needed for the particular result, rather
    28→than requiring affine independence of the points of a triangle
    29→unnecessarily.
    30→
    31→## References
    32→
    33→* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_of_cosines
    34→* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pons_asinorum
    35→* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sum_of_angles_of_a_triangle
    36→* https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Law_of_sines
    37→

    42→noncomputable section

    44→open scoped CharZero Real RealInnerProductSpace
    45→
    46→namespace InnerProductGeometry
    47→
    48→/-!
    49→### Geometrical results on triangles in real inner product spaces
    50→
    51→This section develops some results on (possibly degenerate) triangles
    52→in real inner product spaces, where those definitions and results can
    53→most conveniently be developed in terms of vectors and then used to
    54→deduce corresponding results for Euclidean affine spaces.
    55→-/
    56→
    57→variable {V : Type*} [NormedAddCommGroup V] [InnerProductSpace ℝ V]
    58→
    59→/-- **Law of cosines** (cosine rule), vector angle form. -/
    60→theorem norm_sub_sq_eq_norm_sq_add_norm_sq_sub_two_mul_norm_mul_norm_mul_cos_angle (x y : V) :

def IsIsosceles {α : Type*} [Dist α] (p q r : α) : Prop :=
  dist p q = dist q r ∨ dist q r = dist r p ∨ dist r p = dist p q

nonrec def Set.IsIsosceles {α : Type} [Dist α] (A : Set α) :=
  Nonempty A ∧ A.Triplewise (IsIsosceles · · ·)
